title: CI/CD with GitHub Actions
description: Automate your entire development pipeline -- from linting and testing to building Docker images and deploying to production -- using GitHub Actions workflows.
keyPoints: CI (Continuous Integration) automatically runs linters and tests on every push so broken code never reaches the main branch
CD (Continuous Deployment) automatically deploys your application when code is merged to main
Workflow files live in .github/workflows/ and use YAML syntax to define triggers, jobs, and steps
Matrix builds test your code across multiple versions of Node.js or Python simultaneously
Caching dependencies (node_modules, pip) can reduce CI run times by 50% or more
Branch protection rules enforce that CI must pass before a pull request can be merged

blocks.heading: What is CI/CD?
blocks.body: Imagine you are working on a team building a house. Without a system, every contractor just does their own thing -- the plumber installs pipes that do not align with the electrician's wiring, and nobody notices until the walls are sealed. CI/CD is like having an inspector check every contractor's work before it goes into the house.

**Continuous Integration (CI)** means every time a developer pushes code, an automated system immediately runs linters, tests, and builds to verify the code works. If anything fails, the developer gets feedback in minutes, not days.

**Continuous Deployment (CD)** takes it one step further. Once the code passes all checks, it is automatically deployed to production. No manual steps, no waiting for someone to press a button on a Friday afternoon.

cards.title: Catch Bugs Early
cards.body: Tests run on every push. A bug introduced in a morning commit is caught before lunch, not discovered in production next week.

cards.title: Consistent Quality
cards.body: Every pull request goes through the same checks. No more "it works on my machine" because CI runs in a clean environment.

cards.title: Ship Faster
cards.body: Automated deployments mean code goes from merge to production in minutes. No more release day anxiety.

blocks.heading: Workflow File Anatomy
blocks.body: A GitHub Actions workflow is a YAML file that lives in the `.github/workflows/` directory. Every workflow has three main sections: a **name**, **triggers** (when it runs), and **jobs** (what it does). Let us break down each piece.

blocks.description: A minimal workflow that shows the basic structure. This runs on every push to main and every pull request.
blocks.code:
# Every workflow starts with a name
name: CI

# Triggers: when should this workflow run?
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]          # Run when code is pushed to main
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]          # Run when a PR targets main

# Jobs: what should the workflow do?
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest    # Use a fresh Ubuntu VM

    steps:
      # Step 1: Check out the code
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # Step 2: Set up the runtime
      - name: Set up Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '22'

      # Step 3: Install dependencies
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      # Step 4: Run your checks
      - name: Lint
        run: npm run lint

      - name: Test
        run: npm test

      - name: Build
        run: npm run build

blocks.heading: Workflow YAML Keywords
rows: Display name for the workflow in GitHub UI
Defines what events trigger the workflow
Groups of steps that run on a fresh VM
Operating system for the job's VM
Individual commands or actions within a job
Reference a reusable action from the marketplace
Pass inputs to a reusable action
Declare job dependencies (run after another job)
Conditional execution of a step or job

blocks.heading: Trigger Types
blocks.body: GitHub Actions supports many trigger types beyond push and pull_request. Here are the most useful ones for a typical project.

blocks.description: Examples of different trigger configurations -- pick the ones that match your workflow.
blocks.code:
# Run on push to specific branches
on:
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
    paths:                       # Only run when these files change
      - 'frontend/**'
      - 'package.json'

---
# Run on pull request events
on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, reopened]
    branches: [main]

---
# Run on a schedule (cron syntax, UTC time)
on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 9 * * 1'          # Every Monday at 9 AM UTC

---
# Manual trigger with inputs
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      environment:
        description: 'Deploy target'
        required: true
        default: 'staging'
        type: choice
        options:
          - staging
          - production

blocks.heading: Environment Variables and Secrets
blocks.body: Workflows often need sensitive values like API keys, database passwords, or deploy tokens. Never put these in your YAML file. Instead, store them as GitHub Secrets (Settings > Secrets and variables > Actions) and reference them with the `secrets` context.

blocks.description: How to use environment variables and secrets in your workflow steps.
blocks.code:
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    # Job-level environment variables (available to all steps)
    env:
      NODE_ENV: production
      NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL: https://api.example.com

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Build with env vars
        run: npm run build
        env:
          # Step-level env var (overrides job-level)
          BUILD_ID: ${{ github.sha }}

      - name: Deploy to server
        run: |
          echo "Deploying commit ${{ github.sha }}"
          ./deploy.sh
        env:
          # Secrets are masked in logs (shown as ***)
          DEPLOY_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.DEPLOY_TOKEN }}
          DATABASE_URL: ${{ secrets.DATABASE_URL }}

blocks.title: Never hardcode secrets in YAML files
blocks.body: Your workflow files are committed to git and visible to anyone with repo access. Always use GitHub Secrets for passwords, tokens, and API keys. Secrets are encrypted at rest and masked in log output.

blocks.heading: Matrix Builds
blocks.body: Matrix builds let you run the same job across multiple configurations simultaneously. This is how you test that your code works on Node 18, 20, and 22, or on both Ubuntu and macOS, without writing separate jobs.

blocks.description: A matrix build that tests across multiple Node.js and Python versions in parallel.
blocks.code:
name: Matrix Test

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    strategy:
      matrix:
        node-version: [18, 20, 22]
        python-version: ['3.10', '3.11', '3.12']
      fail-fast: false       # Continue other matrix jobs even if one fails

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Node.js ${{ matrix.node-version }}
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ matrix.node-version }}

      - name: Set up Python ${{ matrix.python-version }}
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}

      - name: Install and test frontend
        run: cd frontend && npm ci && npm test

      - name: Install and test backend
        run: cd backend && pip install -r requirements.txt && pytest

blocks.heading: Caching Dependencies
blocks.body: Installing dependencies from scratch on every CI run wastes time and bandwidth. Caching stores your node_modules or pip packages between runs so they only need to be downloaded when package.json or requirements.txt changes.

blocks.description: Caching both npm and pip dependencies to dramatically speed up CI runs.
blocks.code:
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # Node.js with built-in cache support
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '22'
          cache: 'npm'                               # Built-in npm cache
          cache-dependency-path: frontend/package-lock.json

      # Python with manual cache configuration
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'

      - name: Cache pip dependencies
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ~/.cache/pip
          key: ${{ runner.os }}-pip-${{ hashFiles('backend/requirements.txt') }}
          restore-keys: |
            ${{ runner.os }}-pip-

      - name: Install frontend
        run: cd frontend && npm ci            # Cache hit = ~5s instead of ~30s

      - name: Install backend
        run: cd backend && pip install -r requirements.txt

blocks.heading: Complete Lint and Test Workflow
blocks.body: Here is a production-ready workflow that lints and tests both the frontend and backend. This is the workflow that runs on every pull request to prevent broken code from being merged.

blocks.description: A complete lint-and-test workflow for a full-stack project with both frontend (Next.js) and backend (Flask).
blocks.code:
name: Lint & Test

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  frontend:
    name: Frontend Checks
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '22'
          cache: 'npm'
          cache-dependency-path: frontend/package-lock.json

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci
        working-directory: frontend

      - name: Run ESLint
        run: npm run lint
        working-directory: frontend

      - name: Run TypeScript type check
        run: npx tsc --noEmit
        working-directory: frontend

      - name: Run Jest tests with coverage
        run: npm test -- --coverage --ci
        working-directory: frontend

      - name: Upload coverage report
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: frontend-coverage
          path: frontend/coverage/
          retention-days: 7

  backend:
    name: Backend Checks
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'

      - name: Cache pip
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ~/.cache/pip
          key: ${{ runner.os }}-pip-${{ hashFiles('backend/requirements.txt') }}

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install -r requirements.txt
        working-directory: backend

      - name: Run flake8 linting
        run: |
          pip install flake8
          flake8 . --max-line-length=120 --exclude=__pycache__
        working-directory: backend

      - name: Run pytest
        run: |
          pip install pytest
          pytest -v --tb=short
        working-directory: backend

      - name: Seed database (verify seed data is valid)
        run: |
          FLASK_APP=app:create_app python -m flask seed-db
        working-directory: backend

blocks.heading: Build and Deploy Workflow
blocks.body: This workflow runs after the lint-and-test workflow passes. It builds the production application and deploys it. The `needs` keyword ensures it only runs if CI succeeds.

blocks.description: A deployment workflow that builds the frontend, packages the backend, and deploys to a server. Only runs on pushes to main after CI passes.
blocks.code:
name: Build & Deploy

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  # Run tests first
  test:
    uses: ./.github/workflows/lint-and-test.yml

  # Build only if tests pass
  build:
    needs: test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      image_tag: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }}

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '22'
          cache: 'npm'
          cache-dependency-path: frontend/package-lock.json

      - name: Build frontend
        run: |
          cd frontend
          npm ci
          npm run build

      - name: Upload build artifacts
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: frontend-build
          path: frontend/.next/
          retention-days: 1

  # Deploy only if build passes
  deploy:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production     # Requires manual approval if configured

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Download build artifacts
        uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: frontend-build
          path: frontend/.next/

      - name: Deploy to server
        run: |
          echo "Deploying to production..."
          # Example: rsync to a server
          # rsync -avz --delete frontend/.next/ user@server:/app/frontend/.next/
          # Or: deploy with Docker (see Docker workflow below)
        env:
          DEPLOY_KEY: ${{ secrets.DEPLOY_KEY }}

blocks.heading: Docker Build and Publish Workflow
blocks.body: If your application runs in Docker containers, you need a workflow that builds the Docker images, tags them, and pushes them to a container registry like GitHub Container Registry (GHCR). A full-stack project has multiple images (frontend, backend), so the workflow should build **all** of them consistently. Using a matrix strategy keeps the workflow DRY, and adding QEMU enables multi-platform builds for both `amd64` (standard servers) and `arm64` (AWS Graviton, Apple Silicon, Raspberry Pi).

blocks.description: A complete Docker workflow that builds multi-platform images for both frontend and backend, and pushes them to GitHub Container Registry with distinctive prefixed names.
blocks.code:
name: Docker Build & Publish

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    tags: ['v*']               # Also run on version tags like v1.0.0

jobs:
  docker-push:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      packages: write          # Required to push to GHCR

    # Matrix strategy builds both images with the same steps.
    # No build-args needed -- the frontend Dockerfile bakes a placeholder,
    # and docker-entrypoint.sh seds in the real URL at container start.
    strategy:
      matrix:
        include:
          - image: myapp-backend
            context: ./backend
          - image: myapp-frontend
            context: ./frontend

    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # QEMU enables cross-platform builds (arm64 on an amd64 runner)
      - name: Set up QEMU
        uses: docker/setup-qemu-action@v3

      - name: Set up Docker Buildx
        uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3

      - name: Log in to GitHub Container Registry
        uses: docker/login-action@v3
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Extract metadata (tags, labels)
        id: meta
        uses: docker/metadata-action@v5
        with:
          images: ghcr.io/${{ github.repository_owner }}/${{ matrix.image }}
          tags: |
            type=sha
            type=raw,value=latest

      - name: Build and push image
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
        with:
          context: ${{ matrix.context }}
          push: true
          platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64
          tags: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }}
          labels: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.labels }}
          cache-from: type=gha
          cache-to: type=gha,mode=max

blocks.title: Why prefix image names?
blocks.body: Without a prefix, images like `frontend` and `backend` are generic and easy to confuse with other projects. Prefixing with your project name (e.g., `myapp-frontend`, `myapp-backend`) makes them immediately identifiable in your container registry.

blocks.heading: Multi-Platform Build Components
rows: Lets an amd64 GitHub runner build arm64 images
Required for multi-platform builds and advanced caching
Produces a single manifest that works on Intel/AMD and ARM servers
Uses GitHub Actions cache for Docker layers
Speeds up subsequent builds by reusing unchanged layers
Runtime sed script in the frontend image
Replaces a build-time placeholder with the real NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL at container start -- one image works in any environment

blocks.heading: Branch Protection Rules
blocks.body: Workflows are only useful if they are enforced. Branch protection rules prevent anyone from pushing directly to main or merging a pull request until all CI checks pass.

blocks.heading: Setting Up Branch Protection
items: Go to your repository on GitHub and click Settings
Click Branches in the left sidebar
Enter main as the branch name pattern
Enable Require a pull request before merging
Enable Require status checks to pass before merging
Search for and select your CI job names (e.g., Frontend Checks, Backend Checks)
Enable Require branches to be up to date before merging
Click Create to save the rule

blocks.title: Why require up-to-date branches?
blocks.body: Without this setting, a PR could pass CI, then another PR gets merged to main, and the first PR's code might conflict with the new changes. Requiring up-to-date branches ensures CI runs against the latest main before merging.

blocks.heading: Reusable Workflows
blocks.body: As your project grows, you will have similar CI logic across multiple workflows. Reusable workflows let you define a workflow once and call it from other workflows, just like functions in code.

blocks.description: A reusable workflow that any other workflow can call to set up Node.js and run tests.
blocks.code:
# This workflow can be called by other workflows
name: Reusable Node.js Test

on:
  workflow_call:                 # This makes it callable
    inputs:
      node-version:
        required: false
        type: string
        default: '22'
      working-directory:
        required: false
        type: string
        default: 'frontend'

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ inputs.node-version }}
          cache: 'npm'
          cache-dependency-path: ${{ inputs.working-directory }}/package-lock.json

      - run: npm ci
        working-directory: ${{ inputs.working-directory }}

      - run: npm run lint
        working-directory: ${{ inputs.working-directory }}

      - run: npm test -- --ci
        working-directory: ${{ inputs.working-directory }}

blocks.description: A workflow that calls the reusable workflow above, keeping the main workflow file clean and simple.
blocks.code:
name: Main Pipeline

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  frontend-test:
    uses: ./.github/workflows/reusable-node-test.yml
    with:
      node-version: '22'
      working-directory: 'frontend'

  deploy:
    needs: frontend-test
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: echo "Deploying to production..."

blocks.heading: Artifact Uploads
blocks.body: Sometimes you need to save files from a CI run -- coverage reports, build outputs, test screenshots. Artifacts let you upload files during a workflow and download them later from the GitHub UI or in subsequent jobs.

blocks.description: Uploading test results and build artifacts so they can be downloaded from the GitHub Actions UI.
blocks.code:
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '22'

      - run: npm ci && npm test -- --coverage
        working-directory: frontend

      # Upload coverage report (always, even if tests fail)
      - name: Upload coverage
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        if: always()
        with:
          name: coverage-report
          path: frontend/coverage/
          retention-days: 14

      # Upload Playwright screenshots on failure
      - name: Upload test screenshots
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        if: failure()
        with:
          name: test-screenshots
          path: frontend/test-results/
          retention-days: 7

blocks.heading: Common issues and how to fix them
items: Permission denied: Add the required permissions block to your job (e.g., packages: write for GHCR)
Cache miss every time: Make sure your cache key includes the hash of the lockfile, not the requirements file content
Tests pass locally but fail in CI: CI uses a clean environment -- check for missing environment variables or files
Workflow not triggering: Verify the branch name in your 'on' triggers matches exactly (main vs master)
npm ci fails: Ensure package-lock.json is committed to git and matches package.json
Docker build fails: Check that the Dockerfile path in context is correct relative to the repo root
Secrets not available: Secrets are not passed to workflows triggered by pull requests from forks (security measure)

blocks.heading: GitHub Actions Cheat Sheet
rows: Run a step only on failure
Run steps in a specific directory

blocks.title: Your CI/CD pipeline is ready
blocks.body: You now have everything you need to set up automated linting, testing, building, and deployment with GitHub Actions. Start with the lint-and-test workflow on your next project, then add deployment workflows as your project matures. Remember: the best CI pipeline is one that your team actually trusts and uses.

references.title: Continuous Delivery: Reliable Software Releases through Build, Test, and Deployment Automation
